title: See the World Through AI Eyes

modules.title: Who Built This Algorithm — and Why?
modules.sub: Tracing the people, money, and motives behind AI systems
modules.description: Learners investigate who funds, designs, and deploys AI tools — and how those choices shape what the system does. You analyse real examples to uncover hidden incentives and practise asking the first critical question any AI investigator needs: whose agenda does this serve?

modules.title: Reading AI Outputs Like a Detective
modules.sub: Spotting patterns, gaps, and bias in what AI produces
modules.description: You put on a detective's mindset and closely examine the outputs of AI-powered systems — from search results to content recommendations — looking for what is emphasised, what is missing, and what that reveals. By the end you have a personal checklist of red-flag signals to carry into any future AI encounter.

modules.title: When Systems Fail: Real Case Studies
modules.sub: Learning from AI decisions that caused real-world harm
modules.description: Working through four documented case studies — including facial recognition errors and biased hiring tools — learners map out exactly how each failure happened and who bore the consequences. You then debate what a fairer design could have looked like and what accountability should follow when AI gets it wrong.

modules.title: Your AI Audit: Pick a Tool, Judge It
modules.sub: Applying your investigator framework to a self-chosen AI tool
modules.description: You choose any AI-powered tool from your own life and run it through the structured audit framework built across the course — examining its creators, outputs, and impact on different groups. The module culminates in a written audit report that makes a clear, evidence-based judgement about who the tool serves and who it may harm.

synopsis: Investigative journalists, civil rights advocates, and skeptical teenagers all need the same skill: the ability to look at an AI-powered system and ask hard questions about who it serves and who it harms. This course puts you in the role of an AI investigator working through four real-world scenarios where the stakes are high and the answers are not obvious.
rationale: Older teens are forming lifelong habits around AI-mediated information — news feeds, search results, content moderation — but lack the analytical literacy to interrogate these systems rather than simply consume their outputs.
learning_outcome: Students can apply a structured critical framework to any AI-powered system they encounter, identify whose interests it reflects, and produce a written audit of a real tool they choose themselves.
